steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Rewrite the second term with denominator k: $42ak = \frac{42ak^{2}}{k}$.

choices.elimination:
• This would be correct only if $42ak = \frac{42a}{k}$, i.e., if $k = \frac{1}{k}$, which is only true when $k=1$.
  • For general $k \gt 0$, the second term is $\frac{42ak^{2}}{k}$, not $\frac{42a}{k}$.
  • Eliminated because the $k^{2}$ factor in the numerator is dropped.

• This simplifies to $84ak$, which equals $2 \times 42ak$ — it ignores the $\frac{42a}{k}$ term entirely.
  • Both terms must be combined, not just one of them doubled.
  • Eliminated because the first term $\frac{42a}{k}$ is absent.

• Expanding: $\frac{42a(k+1)}{k} = \frac{42ak + 42a}{k} = 42a + \frac{42a}{k}$.
  • This equals the original only if $42ak = 42a$, i.e., $k = 1$. It doesn't hold for general $k$.
  • Eliminated because the correct factor is $(k^{2}+1)$, not $(k+1)$.

• Convert the second term: $42ak = \frac{42ak^{2}}{k}$.
  • Add: $\frac{42a}{k} + \frac{42ak^{2}}{k} = \frac{42a(1+k^{2})}{k} = \frac{42a(k^{2}+1)}{k}$.
  • This matches exactly. That confirms D is correct.
